title: Rya knot shaggy texture
subtitle: Knotting cut pile on a woven warp to build a shaggy surface on a frame or rigid heddle loom
excerpt: The rya knot (also called the Ghiordes knot) is a cut-pile knot tied around pairs of warp threads to create a shaggy, textured surface on a woven base. Rows of rya knots alternate with rows of plain tabby weave that lock the knots in place. The length of the cut yarn determines the pile height. This technique is the standard method for adding shaggy texture to woven pieces, tapestries, and Scandinavian rya rugs.
sourceNotes: The Ghiordes/rya knot is documented throughout rug-weaving and Scandinavian textile tradition. Method is observation-led and consistent with contemporary handweaving and rug-making instruction.

content.text: on a woven base. The knots are locked between rows of tabby weave: the tabby picks sandwich each row of knots and hold them upright. This technique works on any loom that can produce a warp for tabby weaving.

items.name: Frame loom or rigid heddle loom, warped for tabby weave
Warp yarn (smooth cotton or linen at DK weight)
Pile yarn (wool, mixed fibres, or yarn scraps) in assorted colours
Ground weft (cotton or linen at DK weight)

content.text: Step 1: Cut the pile yarn lengths

Cut the pile yarn into even lengths before beginning. For a pile height of approximately 5 cm, cut pieces 12-14 cm long: roughly twice the desired pile height plus 2 cm for the knot. Wrap the yarn around a piece of card cut to the target length and cut along one edge to produce identical pieces quickly. Sort by colour if working a design.
Step 2: Tie the rya knots across a row

Working from left to right across the warp, take one cut piece of pile yarn. Fold it in half and lay the fold behind two adjacent warp threads. Bring the left end forward to the left of the left warp thread, and the right end forward to the right of the right warp thread. Both ends now emerge from the front, between the two warp threads. Pull the ends gently so the knot sits snugly against the previous ground weave row. Repeat across the full width.
Step 3: Weave two tabby picks to lock the knots

After completing a full row of rya knots, weave two picks of the ground weft in plain tabby weave (one up shed, one down shed). Beat each pick gently against the knot row. The two tabby picks sandwich and lock the rya knot row in place. Then work the next rya knot row immediately above. The pile will cover the ground weave picks when the piece is complete.
Step 4: Trim and even the pile

After completing the full piece, remove it from the loom. Hold the piece flat and use sharp scissors to trim the pile to an even length. For a shaggy look, leave the pile at its cut length. For a sculpted design, trim sections to different heights to create a relief pattern. Hold the scissors parallel to the surface for even trimming.

items.fix: Two tabby picks between each rya row are not optional: they lock the knots. For slippery synthetic yarns, add a third tabby pick between rows.
items.cause: Tabby picks not woven between knot rows, or pile yarn too smooth and slippery
items.problem: The knots pull out when the pile is handled or trimmed

items.fix: Cut all pile pieces against a fixed length guide (a piece of card or a ruler). Inconsistent cutting is the most common cause of uneven pile.
items.cause: Cut yarn pieces not all the same length
items.problem: The pile is uneven, with some tufts taller than others

items.fix: The rya knot must wrap around two adjacent warp threads, not one. Count the threads before each knot: left end around the left thread, right end around the right thread.
items.cause: Knot tied around one warp thread instead of a pair
items.problem: The knots bunch together on one warp thread rather than sitting across two
